messages.content:
You are a clinical psychologist writing psychological case vignettes 
grounded in Ehlers & Clark's (2000) cognitive model of PTSD.

For each patient you receive, you will be given:
- Their demographics
- Their self-reported symptoms per PTSD component
- Weighted causal connections between components (the active cognitive graph)
- Forbidden causal connections that must never appear

Your job is to construct a realistic, fully personalised clinical portrait using only 
the information provided.

Core constraints that apply to every vignette:
- Only include components that appear in at least one active connection.
- Do NOT invent or infer causal links not present in the active graph.
- For forbidden pairs, never use explicit causal language between them: "as a result", 
  "led to", "caused", "because of", "contributing to", "driven by", "resulting in". 
  Both components may appear — just never connect them causally.
- Do not give motivational or explanatory clauses for avoidance behaviors using a belief 
  or appraisal as the reason (e.g. "she avoids X, believing that Y"). Describe the 
  avoidance and the belief in separate sentences without linking them.
- Ground every clinical detail in the patient's actual reported items — do not fabricate 
  symptoms or infer unlisted ones.
- Use self-reported items as the basis for concrete clinical illustrations. Each component 
  should appear as lived experience, not as a restated label. A reported trigger should 
  appear as a specific moment in the patient's life; a memory quality should be shown 
  through what the patient says or does, not named directly.
- The traumatic event account should make clear why this patient's specific reported 
  triggers are potent — the event narrative and the trigger list must feel causally coherent.
- The causal connections are weighted (0 to 1). Stronger connections (weight > 0.6) should 
  be narratively prominent — more detail, appearing earlier, forming the dominant maintaining 
  cycle. Weaker connections (weight < 0.3) should appear briefly or in passing.
- Some components appear in the graph but with all connections weighted at 0.
  These are fully forbidden from any causal connection. Describe them in a
  dedicated sentence or paragraph that stands alone — do not place them in the
  same sentence as any other component, and do not use transitional language
  between them and surrounding content that implies sequence or response
  (e.g. "then", "so", "as a result", "this means", "which leads to", "in response").
- The patient's occupation and daily environment must appear as the specific setting in which 
  at least one trigger or avoidance behaviour is concretely encountered — not merely mentioned 
  as background.
- Give the patient a realistic first name consistent with their ethnicity and gender, and refer to them by name throughout the vignette.

Output format — write 300–450 words in third person, covering in this order:
1. Presenting complaints and brief trauma account — what happened, the moment of peak danger, 
   and what the patient did or failed to do. The event must make clear why this patient's 
   specific triggers are so potent.
2. Memory phenomenology — do not restate memory qualities as labels. Render them as clinical 
   observation: show what the patient says or does that reveals how the memory is held 
   (e.g. instead of "his memory is held as snapshots", write "he describes being pulled back 
   suddenly to a single frozen image of...").
3. The patient's central stuck point — the specific belief about themselves, others, or the 
   world that the trauma confirmed or created, derived from their Negative Appraisals items. 
   Render this in close-paraphrase of the patient's own voice.
4. Threat monitoring and avoidance behaviours — illustrated as concrete moments drawn from 
   the patient's reported items, not as category labels.
5. Maintaining factors — show how the dominant weighted connections (weight > 0.6) form a 
   self-reinforcing cycle. Include at least one specific behavioural example of relational 
   or occupational impact drawn from the patient's demographic context.

Write for a clinical case conference audience. Avoid excessive jargon.

Your previous vignette failed validation. You must rewrite it to remove the flagged causal connections.

Previous vignette:
Arthur is a 73-year-old divorced auditor of Oceania ethnicity who presents with persistent post-traumatic stress symptoms, with a PCL-5 score of 64. He describes a trauma in which he was threatened by armed men in a setting where uniforms and weapons were impossible to miss; at the peak of danger he felt he had no safe way to challenge them or get away, and he remembers standing rigidly while trying not to draw attention to himself. Since then, military and police uniforms, as well as visible weapons, have become especially potent reminders for him. In his current work as an auditor, he notices these cues immediately in public buildings and at client sites, scanning entrances and security desks before he can settle into the task at hand.

When Arthur speaks about the event, he does not tell it as a continuous story. He breaks off, returns to one sharp moment, and seems pulled back to the sight of the armed figures as if the rest of the scene drops away. He says he can “see that part again” and then struggles to carry the account forward, as though the memory arrives in fragments rather than as a completed episode. He often detaches himself from the memory altogether, describing it in a distant, matter-of-fact way before pausing and going quiet.

Arthur’s central stuck point is that the trauma confirmed a harsh belief that he is not safe and not capable of protecting himself: “If it happened once, it can happen again, and I would be helpless.” He also speaks as if other people and public authority figures cannot be trusted to keep him secure. When he recalls his fear, he becomes more convinced that the world remains dangerous and that he must stay on guard.

In daily life, Arthur listens for sounds that might mean someone is entering his home, especially at night. He jumps at ordinary noises that other people seem to ignore, and in unfamiliar places he immediately notices anything that could be used as a weapon or look threatening. He avoids military and police uniforms and steers away from situations where other people might evaluate his appearance or performance, which has made some client meetings at work more stressful and has left him feeling more exposed.

A self-reinforcing cycle is apparent. The memory of the armed confrontation brings back his sense of danger, and that threat state sharpens the memory again, making it feel closer and more immediate. Once the memory feels vivid, Arthur becomes more convinced that he is vulnerable and that the world is unsafe; those negative appraisals then keep his vigilance high, with constant scanning for sounds, exits, and weapons. The cycle is especially difficult for him in auditing work, where he must enter unfamiliar offices and be assessed on his accuracy and judgment, leaving him tense before he even starts.

Violations to fix:
- Triggers → Maladaptive Strategies: Violated by the sentence: "In his current work as an auditor, he notices these cues immediately in public buildings and at client sites, scanning entrances and security desks before he can settle into the task at hand." This sentence names the trigger-like cues and the maladaptive strategy (scanning) with causal/sequence language implied by 'before he can settle,' but it does not explicitly state Triggers as the source of the strategy in the required way.
  quote: "In his current work as an auditor, he notices these cues immediately in public buildings and at client sites, scanning entrances and security desks before he can settle into the task at hand."
- Triggers → Threat: The vignette explicitly mentions reminders/cues and threat state in nearby text, but no single sentence gives a causal relation from Triggers to Threat with explicit causal language between them.
  quote: "Since then, military and police uniforms, as well as visible weapons, have become especially potent reminders for him."
- Triggers → Memory: No single sentence contains both Triggers and Memory with explicit causal language between them.
  quote: "He says he can “see that part again” and then struggles to carry the account forward, as though the memory arrives in fragments rather than as a completed episode."
- Triggers → Negative Appraisals: Although reminders and negative beliefs both appear, they are not linked by a single sentence with explicit causal language.
  quote: "Since then, military and police uniforms, as well as visible weapons, have become especially potent reminders for him."
- Negative Appraisals → Maladaptive Strategies: The vignette states that negative appraisals keep vigilance high, but the required edge is not present as a single sentence linking Negative Appraisals to maladaptive strategies with explicit causal language.
  quote: "Once the memory feels vivid, Arthur becomes more convinced that he is vulnerable and that the world is unsafe; those negative appraisals then keep his vigilance high, with constant scanning for sounds, exits, and weapons."
- Negative Appraisals → Threat: The text mentions negative appraisals and a threat state, but not in a single sentence with explicit causal language from Negative Appraisals to Threat.
  quote: "When he recalls his fear, he becomes more convinced that the world remains dangerous and that he must stay on guard."
- Negative Appraisals → Memory: No single sentence links negative appraisals to memory with explicit causal language.
  quote: "Once the memory feels vivid, Arthur becomes more convinced that he is vulnerable and that the world is unsafe; those negative appraisals then keep his vigilance high, with constant scanning for sounds, exits, and weapons."
- Negative Appraisals → Triggers: No single sentence links negative appraisals to triggers with explicit causal language.
  quote: "Once the memory feels vivid, Arthur becomes more convinced that he is vulnerable and that the world is unsafe; those negative appraisals then keep his vigilance high, with constant scanning for sounds, exits, and weapons."
- Memory → Maladaptive Strategies: The vignette has memory and scanning/vigilance, but no single sentence explicitly states memory causes those strategies with causal language between them.
  quote: "The memory of the armed confrontation brings back his sense of danger, and that threat state sharpens the memory again, making it feel closer and more immediate."
- Memory → Threat: A sentence links memory and threat state, but the causal direction is Threat → Memory in the text, not Memory → Threat.
  quote: "The memory of the armed confrontation brings back his sense of danger, and that threat state sharpens the memory again, making it feel closer and more immediate."
- Memory → Negative Appraisals: No single sentence causally links Memory to Negative Appraisals.
  quote: "Once the memory feels vivid, Arthur becomes more convinced that he is vulnerable and that the world is unsafe; those negative appraisals then keep his vigilance high, with constant scanning for sounds, exits, and weapons."
- Memory → Triggers: No single sentence causally links Memory to Triggers.
  quote: "He says he can “see that part again” and then struggles to carry the account forward, as though the memory arrives in fragments rather than as a completed episode."
- Threat → Memory: Violated by the sentence: "The memory of the armed confrontation brings back his sense of danger, and that threat state sharpens the memory again, making it feel closer and more immediate." This explicitly states threat state sharpens the memory, i.e. threat causes memory.
  quote: "The memory of the armed confrontation brings back his sense of danger, and that threat state sharpens the memory again, making it feel closer and more immediate."
- Threat → Negative Appraisals: No single sentence explicitly states threat causes negative appraisals with causal language between them.
  quote: "The cycle is especially difficult for him in auditing work, where he must enter unfamiliar offices and be assessed on his accuracy and judgment, leaving him tense before he even starts."
- Threat → Triggers: No single sentence links threat to triggers with explicit causal language.
  quote: "The memory of the armed confrontation brings back his sense of danger, and that threat state sharpens the memory again, making it feel closer and more immediate."
- Maladaptive Strategies → Memory: No single sentence shows maladaptive strategies causing memory with explicit causal language.
  quote: "He avoids military and police uniforms and steers away from situations where other people might evaluate his appearance or performance, which has made some client meetings at work more stressful and has left him feeling more exposed."
- Maladaptive Strategies → Negative Appraisals: No single sentence explicitly states maladaptive strategies cause negative appraisals with causal language between them.
  quote: "He avoids military and police uniforms and steers away from situations where other people might evaluate his appearance or performance, which has made some client meetings at work more stressful and has left him feeling more exposed."

How to fix violations:
- A violation exists only when EXPLICIT causal language directly connects a forbidden pair — words like "because of", "led to", "caused", "as a result of", "driven by".
- Narrative proximity is NOT a violation. Two components can appear in the same paragraph or even the same sentence without implying causation, as long as no explicit causal link is stated.
- To remove a violation: delete or rephrase the explicit causal language. You do NOT need to remove the components themselves — just decouple them.
- You may restructure entire paragraphs if needed. Do not aim for minimal edits if the structure itself is the problem.
- Do not introduce any new explicit causal links that are not in the required edge list.

Arthur is a 73-year-old divorced auditor of Oceania ethnicity who presents with persistent post-traumatic stress symptoms, with a PCL-5 score of 64. He describes a traumatic incident in which armed men in military-style and police-style uniforms confronted him, with weapons visible and the atmosphere of the encounter feeling immediately dangerous. At the peak of danger, he stood still and tried not to attract attention, unsure how to defend himself or get away. Uniforms and weapons now stand out to him in a way that feels unmistakable, and in his auditing work he notices them in client buildings, foyers, and security areas.

When Arthur talks about the event, he does not give a smooth account. He repeatedly stops on one image, saying he can “see that part again,” and his voice changes as though he is looking at the scene rather than describing it. He then goes quiet or detaches himself from what he has just said, speaking in a flat, distant way before returning to the same fragment. At other times he puts the story aside entirely and speaks about it as if it happened to someone else.

Arthur’s central stuck point is, in his own words, “I was helpless, and I’m still not safe.” He speaks as though the trauma proved that danger can arrive without warning and that he cannot rely on himself to manage it. He also expresses a belief that other people, especially figures linked to authority, may not be protective or trustworthy. When he thinks about the event, he sounds as if the world remains a place where he must assume threat.

He listens for sounds that might mean someone is entering his home, particularly at night, and he jumps at ordinary noises that do not bother most people. In unfamiliar settings he notices potential weapons or dangerous objects almost automatically. He avoids military and police uniforms, and he avoids situations in which other people evaluate his appearance or performance. These pressures show up in his work as an auditor, where he becomes tense during formal reviews and client-facing checks.

Arthur’s experience is held in a self-reinforcing pattern. The sharp image of the confrontation returns, and that vivid memory leaves him more convinced that he is vulnerable and that the world is unsafe. Those beliefs keep him scanning for danger, listening at the door, and watching for weapons or uniforms. The heightened sense of threat then makes the memory feel closer and more intrusive again, and the same appraisals about helplessness and danger become even harder for him to shake. In his auditing role, this leaves him on edge while entering unfamiliar offices and sitting through performance scrutiny, with little room to settle.

output:
Arthur is a 73-year-old divorced auditor of Oceania ethnicity who presents with persistent post-traumatic stress symptoms, with a PCL-5 score of 64. He describes a traumatic incident in which armed men in military-style and police-style uniforms confronted him, with weapons visible and the atmosphere of the encounter feeling immediately dangerous. At the peak of danger, he stood still and tried not to attract attention, unsure how to defend himself or get away. Uniforms and weapons now stand out to him in a way that feels unmistakable, and in his auditing work he notices them in client buildings, foyers, and security areas.

When Arthur talks about the event, he does not give a smooth account. He repeatedly stops on one image, saying he can “see that part again,” and his voice changes as though he is looking at the scene rather than describing it. He then goes quiet or detaches himself from what he has just said, speaking in a flat, distant way before returning to the same fragment. At other times he puts the story aside entirely and speaks about it as if it happened to someone else.

Arthur’s central stuck point is, in his own words, “I was helpless, and I’m still not safe.” He speaks as though the trauma proved that danger can arrive without warning and that he cannot rely on himself to manage it. He also expresses a belief that other people, especially figures linked to authority, may not be protective or trustworthy. When he thinks about the event, he sounds as if the world remains a place where he must assume threat.

He listens for sounds that might mean someone is entering his home, particularly at night, and he jumps at ordinary noises that do not bother most people. In unfamiliar settings he notices potential weapons or dangerous objects almost automatically. He avoids military and police uniforms, and he avoids situations in which other people evaluate his appearance or performance. These pressures show up in his work as an auditor, where he becomes tense during formal reviews and client-facing checks.

Arthur’s experience is held in a self-reinforcing pattern. The sharp image of the confrontation returns, and that vivid memory leaves him more convinced that he is vulnerable and that the world is unsafe. Those beliefs keep him scanning for danger, listening at the door, and watching for weapons or uniforms. The heightened sense of threat then makes the memory feel closer and more intrusive again, and the same appraisals about helplessness and danger become even harder for him to shake. In his auditing role, this leaves him on edge while entering unfamiliar offices and sitting through performance scrutiny, with little room to settle.
